raw_consumer_statement:
On XX/XX/XXXX, I was charged {$120.00} by a merchant called XXXX XXXX XXXX  for a preorder product ( XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. The merchant claimed the item would be delivered in XX/XX/XXXX. After repeated delays and vague updates, the product was still not delivered as of XX/XX/XXXX 19 months later. 

On XX/XX/XXXX, the merchant stated that delivery would start in XX/XX/XXXX, but this never occurred. Since then, the game developer has publicly ended its relationship with the merchant, meaning the seller is no longer authorized to produce or deliver the item. I contacted the merchant via their website on XX/XX/XXXX, and received no response. They appear to be ignoring customer inquiries while continuing to take money for undeliverable goods. I believe this constitutes fraudulent activity or, at a minimum, deceptive business practices. 

I filed a dispute with American Express on XX/XX/XXXX. Amex denied my dispute on XX/XX/XXXX, citing their internal 60-day limit for billing disputes. I explained this was not a billing error, but a case of merchant fraud and non-fulfillment. Despite this, Amex closed the case again.

Amex is refusing to investigate a clear non-delivery and suspected fraud case, despite my prompt report, even though I had confirmation that the product would never be delivered. I am requesting that the CFPB review both the merchants behavior and Amexs refusal to protect its cardholders from fraudulent transactions.
